meta.title: X164 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes GL-Class X164 engine — OM642 V6 diesel, M273 V8 petrol, OM629 V8 diesel. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes GL-Class X164 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X164 engine typically costs £3,200–£8,000 supply only, depending on engine — the OM642 V6 diesel sits at £3,200–£6,000, the M273 V8 petrol at £4,000–£8,000, and the OM629 V8 diesel at £4,500–£7,500. Fitting adds £780–£1,300 depending on engine complexity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes GL-Class X164 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642.820 V6 diesel (post-2009 GL 350 BlueTEC, 211 bhp) is our top recommendation — it's a proven, well-understood engine that powers millions of Mercedes vehicles. The post-2010 GL 350 CDI (265 bhp) shares the same core reliability with more power. The OM629 V8 diesel is also robust but rarer and more complex.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes GL-Class X164?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler seal failure and exhaust manifold cracking on the OM642 V6 diesel, thermostat and intake manifold actuator failure on the M273 V8 petrol, and secondary air injection pump failure on the M273. The 7G-Tronic transmission (722.9) is generally reliable but requires regular servicing.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes GL-Class X164 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, but maintenance is key. The OM642 V6 diesel is proven and reliable — oil cooler seals and exhaust manifolds are the main wear items. The M273 V8 petrol is robust but complex — thermostat and intake actuator failures are common. The OM629 V8 diesel is reliable but rare, so specialist knowledge is essential.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes GL-Class X164 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright 'avoid', but we'd counsel caution on pre-2009 OM642.940 (GL 320 CDI) without documented oil cooler seal history — the oil cooler is a significant repair. A neglected M273 V8 without thermostat or intake actuator history is also a risk. Budget examples of any X164 require careful inspection.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X164 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil leaks or consumption (OM642 oil cooler seals), poor idle or loss of power (M273 intake actuators), loud noise on cold start (M273 secondary air pump), or injector noise (OM629) are the key warning signs. Check engine lights on any variant should be diagnosed immediately.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes GL-Class X164 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on the car's value. Against a well-kept £7,000 X164, a £3,200–£6,000 OM642 recon is 45–85% — borderline. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon exceeds value — walk away. For the V8, against a £8,000+ GL 500, a £4,000–£8,000 recon is 50–100% — viable only on exceptional examples. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line: value the car first because the engine is often worth more than the car.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes GL-Class X164 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 V6 diesel is proven to exceed 200,000 miles with diligent maintenance — oil cooler seals are the limiting factor. The M273 V8 petrol can reach 180,000+ miles with proper servicing — thermostat and intake actuators are the main wear items. The OM629 V8 diesel is rarer but similarly robust.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X164, X166 and X167?

acceptedAnswer.text: The X164 (2006–2012, this page) is the first-generation GL-Class, built on the W164 platform. The X166 (2012–2015) is the second-generation GL-Class, built on the W166 platform. The X167 GLS (2015–present) is the third-generation, renamed GLS to reflect Mercedes' new naming strategy. Each generation brought significant improvements in technology and refinement.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes GL-Class X164 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (e.g., GL 320 CDI) and approximate registration year (pre- or post-2009 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes GL-Class X164 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation GL-Class full-size SUV — from the OM642 V6 diesel to the 5.5-litre M273 V8 that defines this generation's character. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the 2009 facelift that brought BlueTEC technology to the UK market. Backed by 880+ real UK OM642 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes GL-Class X164 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X164 full-size SUV — including the 2009 facelift that brought BlueTEC technology and revised power outputs.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: OM642: 90% confidence — 880 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries in 2025; well-documented engine with established failure patterns. M273: 85% confidence — well-understood naturally aspirated V8. OM629: 75% confidence — lower-volume V8 diesel with pattern data.

overview.intro: The X164 is the first generation of the Mercedes-Benz GL-Class, introduced in 2006 as the flagship of the Mercedes SUV line. Built on the same platform as the W164 M-Class, it offered three rows of seating and full-size luxury in a seven-passenger package. A 2009 facelift brought revised styling and the introduction of BlueTEC diesel technology to the UK market. The range spanned from the OM642 V6 diesel to the 5.5-litre M273 V8, with the OM629 V8 diesel sitting between them.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz W164/V251 • Predecessor: — (first generation) • Successor: GL-Class X166 (2012–2015) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (X164 is a standalone full-size SUV; shares platform with W164 M-Class and R-Class).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The OM642 V6 diesel engine family generated 880 real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the GL 320 CDI and GL 350 BlueTEC as these ageing full-size SUVs continue to serve family and towing duties.

items.quote: The most powerful iteration of the OM642 V6 diesel offered in the X164 — 265 bhp and 620 Nm of torque in a 2.5-tonne SUV. Oil cooler seal failures and exhaust manifold cracks are its known weak points, but the underlying engine is robust and proven across millions of Mercedes vehicles.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want maximum diesel torque without the V8's fuel bills.

items.quote: The 5.5-litre naturally aspirated V8 delivers 388 bhp and 530 Nm of torque — effortless, smooth, and the engine that defines the GL-Class experience. Thermostat failure and intake manifold actuator issues are its only real demands, but it's a proven, characterful engine.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 refinement and don't mind the fuel bills.

items.quote: The early OM642 V6 diesel is a solid engine, but oil cooler seal failure (located in the V of the engine) is a £1,000+ repair — and earlier cars (pre-2009) have a documented risk of crankshaft main bearing fatigue.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who accept the risks of an early V6 diesel and have service history to prove maintenance.

items.quote: The 5.5-litre V8 is a masterpiece, but it's an ageing, complex engine — thermostat failure, secondary air injection pump failure, and intake manifold actuator issues are all common, and parts are expensive. A full replacement can exceed £8,000.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the flagship V8 and have the budget to maintain it.

items.quote: The post-facelift diesel offers improved emissions technology with the same core OM642 reliability — and significantly lower fuel bills than any V8. It's the sensible choice for a 2.5-tonne SUV.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy and don't need the extra power of the 265 bhp version.

items.quote: The 4.0-litre V8 diesel is often forgotten between the V6 diesel and the 5.5-litre petrol — but it offers 306 bhp and 700 Nm of torque, combining diesel economy with V8 refinement. It's rare, but that's exactly why it's overlooked.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 presence with diesel running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid V8 — reliable but parts are costly.
Flagship V8 — effortless but expensive to maintain.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil cooler seals, exhaust manifold cracks
rows.ownershipVerdict: Early Diesel — solid but watch for oil leaks.
Diesel Performance — more power, same risks.
V8 Diesel — rare, refined, but complex.

economicsRule.title: The X164 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£6,000 OM642 recon against a £4,000–£12,000 X164 sits at 25–150% of the car's value — on sub-£5,000 examples, it often approaches or exceeds our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. The M273 V8 recon at £4,000–£8,000 against a £5,000–£15,000 GL 500 is 25–160% — viable only on well-maintained, higher-value examples. These are ageing, heavy SUVs; value the car first, because the engine can easily be worth more than the car on budget examples.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X164 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GL 320 CDI (OM642) — cheapest diesel recon, best fuel economy.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X164 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GL 500 (M273) — V8 parts and fuel costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X164 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: GL 350 BlueTEC (OM642.820) — modern diesel without the early OM642's risks.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Failure
problems.affectedModels: X164 GL 320 CDI, GL 350 BlueTEC, GL 350 CDI (all OM642 variants).
problems.rootCause: The oil cooler is located in the V of the engine — its seals degrade with age and heat cycling, causing oil to leak externally. The repair requires removing the intake manifold and a host of ancillary parts, making labour costs significant.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil leaks or oil consumption on any OM642-powered X164, have the oil cooler seals inspected — a £1,000–£2,000 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to engine failure.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your X164 GL 320 CDI →

problems.title: OM642 Exhaust Manifold Cracking
problems.affectedModels: X164 GL 320 CDI, GL 350 BlueTEC, GL 350 CDI (all OM642 variants).
problems.rootCause: Extreme temperatures in the exhaust area cause the exhaust manifold to develop cracks or leaks — this can reduce performance and, long-term, damage the turbocharger.
problems.recommendation: If your OM642-powered X164 develops a ticking noise or loss of power, have the exhaust manifold inspected — an £800–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows if turbo damage occurs.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your X164 GL 350 BlueTEC →

problems.title: M273 Thermostat & Intake Manifold Actuator Failure
problems.affectedModels: X164 GL 450, GL 500 / GL 550 (all M273 variants).
problems.rootCause: The map-controlled thermostat is the single most common failure on the M273 — it fails, causing the engine to run cold and reduce fuel economy. Intake manifold actuators also fail, causing poor idle and loss of power.
problems.recommendation: If your GL 450 or GL 500 runs cold or has a poor idle, have the thermostat and intake manifold inspected — a £500–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows if the actuator fails and causes valve damage.

cta.label: Compare M273 engine prices for your X164 GL 500 →

problems.title: M273 Secondary Air Injection Pump Failure
problems.affectedModels: X164 GL 450, GL 500 / GL 550 (all M273 variants).
problems.rootCause: The secondary air injection pump injects fresh air into the exhaust system on cold starts to aid catalytic converter warm-up — with age, these pumps become loud and are prone to failure.
problems.recommendation: If your GL 450 or GL 500 makes a loud noise on cold start, have the secondary air pump inspected — a £400–£800 repair is far cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows if the pump fails and damages the catalytic converters.

cta.label: Compare M273 engine prices for your X164 GL 450 →

replacementCosts.h2: X164 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the X164 shares its platform with the W164 M-Class, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X164 GL-Class only (2006–2012). The X166 GL-Class (2012–2015) and X167 GLS (2015–present) are separate generations. The GL 400 CDI was produced for non-UK markets and is not covered here. Note that the GL 350 CDI (265 bhp) was introduced post-2010 as a higher-output version of the OM642.

mostRequestedEngines: 1. OM642.940/820/822 (GL 320 CDI / GL 350 BlueTEC / GL 350 CDI) — 880 [MB-VERIFIED]
mostCommonFailures: 3. M273 thermostat & intake manifold actuator failure
